title: Chapter 15: Simple Phenomena of Magnetism — Medium

questions.question: What is the name of the process where a magnetic material becomes a magnet when it is placed near a permanent magnet?
options: Induced magnetism
Magnetic conduction
Electrostatic charge
Thermal expansion
questions.explanation:
Induced magnetism is the process where a magnet induces magnetism in a magnetic material (like an iron bar) by being near it or in contact with it.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: An N pole of a bar magnet is brought near to end A of an unmagnetised iron bar. What pole is induced at end A?
options: South pole
North pole
No pole is induced
A North pole and a South pole simultaneously
questions.explanation:
In induction, the pole induced at the nearer end of the magnetic material is always the opposite of the pole of the inducing magnet. Thus, an N pole induces a South pole at the nearer end.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: According to the kinetic particle model (as applied to magnetism), what are 'magnetic domains'?
options: Groups of atoms that behave like tiny magnets
Regions in space where the magnetic field is zero
The physical ends of a bar magnet
The atoms of non-magnetic materials
questions.explanation:
Magnetic materials contain 'magnetic domains,' where each domain behaves like a small magnet. In an unmagnetised bar, these domains point in random directions.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student performs an experiment where paper clips are attracted to a magnet. The first paper clip then attracts a second paper clip. Why does this happen?
options: The first paper clip has become an induced magnet
The second paper clip is a permanent magnet
The force of the original magnet passes through the first clip without changing it
Paper clips are naturally magnets even without a nearby field
questions.explanation:
When the first paper clip is attracted to the magnet, it becomes an induced magnet, which allows it to attract further paper clips.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which of the following would be the best way to verify if a metal bar is a permanent magnet rather than just a magnetic material?
options: Check if it can repel the pole of another known magnet
Check if it is attracted to the N pole of a magnet
Check if it is attracted to the S pole of a magnet
Check if it can pick up iron filings
questions.explanation:
Repulsion is the only sure test for magnetism. A magnetic material will be attracted to both poles of a magnet, but only another magnet can experience repulsion.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Two metal bars, P and Q, are tested with a magnet. Bar P picks up many paper clips but drops them all when the magnet is removed. Bar Q picks up fewer clips but keeps most of them. What are materials P and Q?
options: P: Soft iron; Q: Steel
P: Steel; Q: Soft iron
P: Brass; Q: Iron
P: Copper; Q: Steel
questions.explanation:
Iron (P) is easy to magnetise (picks up many clips) but loses magnetism easily (temporary). Steel (Q) is harder to magnetise (fewer clips initially) but retains it (permanent). 

**Common mistake:** Swapping the properties of iron and steel.

questions.question: Why is soft iron, rather than steel, used for the core of an electromagnet?
options: It is easily magnetised and easily demagnetised
It produces a permanent magnetic field
It is a better conductor of electricity than steel
It is cheaper and more widely available
questions.explanation:
Soft iron is 'magnetically soft,' meaning it responds quickly to the presence or absence of a magnetic field, which is essential for switching an electromagnet on and off. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking steel is used because it makes a 'stronger' permanent electromagnet.

questions.question: Compare the magnetic properties of iron and steel according to the 'Let's Investigate' results.
options: Iron is easier to magnetise but harder to keep magnetised than steel.
Steel is easier to magnetise and keep magnetised than iron.
Iron and steel have identical magnetic properties.
Steel loses its magnetism immediately when the magnet is removed.
questions.explanation:
The source deduction states 'Iron loses its magnetism easily, but steel does not.' Iron is also shown picking up more tacks initially, meaning it is easier to magnetise. 

**Common mistake:** Believing steel is the 'best' at everything; iron is actually easier to magnetise.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies a magnetically soft material and its property?
options: Material: Soft iron; Property: Easy to magnetise
Material: Steel; Property: Difficult to magnetise
Material: Brass; Property: Non-magnetic
Material: Soft iron; Property: Difficult to magnetise
questions.explanation:
Soft iron is the standard example of a magnetically soft material, characterized by being easy to magnetise and demagnetise. 

**Common mistake:** Row B is a correct fact about steel, but steel is magnetically 'hard', not 'soft'.

questions.question: A student wants to make a compass needle. Which material should they use and why?
options: Steel, because it will retain its magnetism as a permanent magnet.
Soft iron, because it can be easily magnetised by the Earth.
Brass, because it is not affected by other magnets.
Copper, because it is a very good conductor.
questions.explanation:
A compass needle must be a permanent magnet so that it remains magnetised without an external source. Steel is the best choice for this. 

**Common mistake:** Choosing iron, which would lose its magnetism and stop working as a compass.

questions.question: A plotting compass is placed near the North pole of a bar magnet. In which direction does the North-seeking end of the compass needle point?
options: Away from the North pole
Towards the North pole
Towards the centre of the magnet
It spins continuously
questions.explanation:
Magnetic field lines go away from the North pole. The compass needle aligns with these lines, so its North pole is repelled by the magnet's North pole.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: When a student replaces a bar magnet with a stronger one, how should the diagram of the magnetic field change?
options: More field lines should be drawn in the same area
The lines should be drawn further apart
The lines should point from South to North
The lines should be perfectly straight
questions.explanation:
A stronger magnetic field is represented by a higher density of field lines, meaning more lines are drawn in the same space.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A compass is placed at the midpoint of a bar magnet, just above its side. According to Figure 15.19, which way will the needle point?
options: Parallel to the magnet, pointing towards the South pole
Parallel to the magnet, pointing towards the North pole
Perpendicular to the magnet, pointing away from it
Perpendicular to the magnet, pointing towards it
questions.explanation:
In the middle of the magnet, the field lines are parallel to the magnet's length and travel from the North end toward the South end.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a 'uniform' magnetic field, such as the one found between two wide, opposite magnetic poles?
options: Straight, parallel, and equally spaced field lines
Circular field lines centered on the poles
Lines that bend away from the centre
A region where there are no field lines
questions.explanation:
A uniform field has the same strength and direction at all points, which is represented by straight, parallel, and equally spaced lines.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student uses iron filings to observe a magnetic field. What is a disadvantage of this method compared to using a plotting compass?
options: Filings do not show the direction of the field
Filings do not show the shape of the field
Filings only work with very weak magnets
Filings can only be used once
questions.explanation:
Iron filings align to show the shape and pattern of the field but they do not have a North/South orientation to show the field's direction.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
